data.title: Touchstone Large Cap Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Touchstone Large Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Large Cap Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived) underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000® Index, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025.

The underperformance of the Quality factor, which the Fund tilts toward, was a headwind to relative performance this quarter. The best performing sectors for the Russell 1000 index were Health Care and Communications Services, while Real Estate and Utilities were the weakest sectors.

During the quarter, sector allocation and stock selection were headwinds to relative performance. An underweight to both Information Technology and Real Estate aided performance, while an underweight Health Care and overweight Financials detracted from performance.

Turning to stock selection, the best performing stocks based on relative performance vs. the benchmark during the quarter included Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (Communications Services sector), FedEx Corp. (FDX) (Industrials sector), and Chubb Ltd. (CB) (Financials sector).

Alphabet was a top performer this quarter following strong core business execution and accelerating adoption of its AI offerings. Growth was broad-based across search, YouTube, and cloud, with AI enhancing search utility and monetization while cloud margins reached new highs. Management continues to balance disciplined cost controls with increased AI investment, reinforcing confidence in long-term returns. We remain attracted to its massive ecosystem scale, sound capital allocation, and clean balance sheet.

FedEx was a top performer after reporting better results led by improved revenue quality, disciplined pricing, and continued cost savings. Despite the freight recession, domestic ground volume has been fairly resilient recently, and margins expanded in the core Express business due to the structural cost reductions. We remain attracted to the strong cash generation, balanced capital allocation, and a solid balance sheet.

Chubb was a strong performer in the fourth quarter, reaching an all-time high, following exceptional earnings that featured record core operating income, industry-leading combined ratios, and robust investment income growth. The company’s diversified portfolio, disciplined underwriting, and aggressive capital returns reinforced confidence in its ability to generate attractive returns, even as the Property and Casualty industry transitions to a more competitive and volatile environment.

The more challenged positions based on relative performance during the quarter included Fiserv Inc. (FISV) (Financials sector), NewMarket Corp. (NEU) (Materials sector), and BlackRock Inc. (BLK) (Financial Services sector).

Fiserv weakness came after ongoing concerns about a slowdown in its small business point-of-sale platform on top of weak third quarter results. The company took a steep guidance cut and announced management and Board changes. The large stock reaction reflects a loss of credibility, increased doubts around the long-term growth outlook, and the realization that a turnaround will require meaningful investment over the medium-term. Given the extent of the reset and diminished confidence in the thesis, we sold our position.

NewMarket was a weaker performer as a softer global environment pressured Petroleum Additives volumes and margins and the Specialty Materials business had lumpy demand, which weighed on results. Lower oil prices reduced operating leverage and drove an unusual restructuring charge as industry capacity increased.

Despite near-term pressure, we continue to view NewMarket as a strong business with disciplined capital allocation, balance sheet flexibility, and strong cash flow generation. Attracted to its best-in- class operations, conservative underwriting, and shareholder- friendly capital allocation philosophy.

BlackRock was a bottom performer during the quarter as investor sentiment was pressured by concerns around private market liquidity, return durability, and higher expenses tied to recent acquisitions. Despite this, fundamentals remain solid, with record AUM, healthy flows, and organic base fee growth well above long-term targets, driven by active ETFs, digital assets, and alternatives. We continue to view BlackRock as a long-term share gainer, and we appreciate the strong balance sheet and steady capital return.

In late December, we trimmed the large position in Alphabet Inc. based on strength and to comply with our stated 10% position limits. We used the funds to increase our position in Republic Services Inc. (RSG) (Industrials sector).

We added to Republic Services as it is an attractive opportunity to increase our exposure in a defensive and resilient business model. The waste management industry has lagged the broader market this year due to cyclical volume weakness in construction, demolition, and the industrial sector. Despite these short term headwinds, Republic Services continues to exhibit operational strength through pricing power and cost controls, which have led to stronger margins. We remain attracted to the high-quality, essential business with strong and stable cash flow generation.

Recently, insiders have bought shares in the open market, which we view as an additional data point to the attractive valuation.

In October, we sold the remaining Fiserv position after it hit our soft stop loss. The doubts over the new long-term algorithm and the durability of its core business are now in question. Prior management’s reliance on low-quality, short-term growth led us to lose confidence in the business. While there is still value in the core business, it is now a turnaround story that will require heavy investment to reset the strategy and restore genuine earnings growth.

In October, we used the excess cash to fund two purchases of existing holdings, Chubb Ltd. and Norfolk Southern Corp. (NSC) (Industrials sector).

We added to our Chubb position after the most recent quarterly report. Results continue to be better than expected and management increased its return on equity target. Loss trends in the industry have stabilized and premiums written should accelerate. Management also accelerated the buyback program and signaled that the stock is trading under its intrinsic value. We remain confident in the attractive nature Chubb business.

We added to our Norfolk Southern position after the shares pulled back despite the pending Union Pacific deal. It is currently trading at a discount to the value of the Union Pacific deal and the rail industry is out of favor. While most recent earnings were softer than expected Norfolk Southern has shown signs of improving its execution and volumes should return incrementally. Lastly, the chairman stepped up and purchased shares in the open market, which we view as a sign of conviction in the business.

A Word About Risk

The Fund may invest a greater percentage of its assets in the securities of a limited number of issuers and may be subject to greater risks. data.title: Touchstone Large Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Highlights

  • Utilizes a bottom-up security selection process that screens potential investments against a proprietary quantitative model for return on capital, earnings to value ratio, free cash flow and return on equity
  • Looks at a company’s corporate governance structure and management incentives to try to ascertain whether or not management’s interests are aligned with shareholder interests
  • Seeks to identify the sources of a company’s competitive advantage as well as what levers management has at its disposal to increase shareholder value
  • Seeks to purchase generally profitable, financially stable large-cap companies that consistently generate high returns on unleveraged operating capital, are run by shareholder-oriented management, and are trading at a discount to their private market value

Market Recap

U.S. equities finished higher for a third consecutive quarter, led by a shift towards a more accommodative monetary policy, resilient consumer spending, and better-than-expected earnings results. Clarity in trade policy also helped ease uncertainties. The 10-year Treasury yield increased to 4.2% amid sticky inflation and delayed expectations of further U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cuts.

Economic data during the quarter were mixed but generally better than expected, reflecting an economy that remained resilient. Real GDP accelerated to a 4.3% annualized pace in the third quarter, providing a strong starting point for fourth quarter, supported by solid consumer spending and business investment. However, estimates are showing a moderation in growth expectations going forward, alongside modest labor market softening and sticky inflation. Affordability pressures and labor market concerns weighed on consumer sentiment. Despite these headwinds and a government shutdown, earnings results remained supportive, reinforcing a constructive fundamental backdrop even as economic momentum began to cool.

Near-term data continues to be delayed by the government shutdown, but the most recent data showed signs of progress being made on the inflation side. Inflation surprised to the downside in November, with core CPI cooling more than expected. The most recent Core PCE, the Fed’s preferred gauge (which excludes food and energy), has not been updated since September. All that said, inflation remains above the Fed’s target.

The labor market data showed unemployment increased and has continued to soften. Labor demand has fallen more than supply, which has been a main driver for the unemployment rate ticking higher, not necessarily due to layoffs. On a positive note, productivity also increased significantly. Unemployment rates were 4.6%. The weakening unemployment numbers continue to be a bigger risk to the overall economy.

Regarding monetary policy, the Fed maintained its restrictive monetary policy but lowered the federal funds rate by 25 basis points to 3.5-3.75% in December due to the rising downside risk of higher unemployment. The three rate cuts this year were all framed as a risk management move to bring policy closer to neutral. The Fed is trying to execute a careful balancing act with the decision to cut rates despite still elevated and potentially persistent inflation. Fed projections now show only one 2026 cut, which may be close to the neutral rate. Additional easing is possible depending on economic data throughout the year.

Lastly, the chairman stepped up and purchased shares in the open market, which we view as a sign of conviction in the business.

Outlook and Conclusion

Despite tariff pressures and a softening labor market, economic growth remained resilient through much of the year, supported by solid consumer spending, productivity, and continued AI-driven investment. Rising productivity increased earnings and fueled broad-based volatility and small-cap equity gains. Elevated valuations now reflect optimistic assumptions around sustained earnings growth and productivity gains. Macro risks have grown more pronounced, including a softening labor market, mounting affordability pressures on consumers, and global policy uncertainty, suggesting markets may be more vulnerable to disappointment as growth slows and policy support becomes less certain.

We expect more modest returns amid elevated valuations, concentrated leadership, and rising macro uncertainty, which increases the risk of volatility and drawdowns. Although we are not forecasting a recession, late-cycle dynamics are becoming more apparent with the uneven economic data. Defensive portfolios like ours remain prudent protection against risks that could still materialize.

In terms of monetary policy, the Fed appears likely to hold interest rates steady at its next meeting, with the markets pricing in one additional rate cut later in 2026. Softer job market data has driven this shift, as policymakers aim to prevent further labor market deterioration while inflation remains above target. As policy moves toward neutral, it is important to note that monetary changes take time to impact the economy.

In terms of the equity market, valuations based on near-term earnings and expectations are elevated. This has reduced the margin for error, as seen with recent earnings, generating limited upside for beats and outsized downside for misses. We believe that modest returns with unpredictable downdrafts appear likely for U.S. equities over the next decade. We see the most compelling opportunities in high-quality companies with durable fundamentals, particularly those that generate strong free cash flow, maintain conservative balance sheets, and return capital to shareholders.

Given we appear to be late in the economic cycle, with growth strong but showing signs of moderation, this environment should favor Quality factors and our portfolios over the next few years. Importantly, when Quality outperforms, it tends to be significant, while underperformance during lagging periods is typically more modest. Longer term, we believe that quality attributes and solid company fundamentals will lead to strong risk adjusted returns.

data.title: 12-Week War To Shave 3% Off Global GDP In A Demand Destruction Event
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As we near two weeks of war, there are some definite trends taking shape that are set to impact the global oil market (CL1:COM) in the next few months, and perhaps beyond. First off, despite reassuring rhetoric, it increasingly looks like both sides are digging in for a prolonged conflict, while at the same time, there are fewer and fewer off-ramps for either side, and neither side looks like it is facing imminent and total defeat. The resulting negative impact on the global oil & gas supply/demand balance is starting to take shape as well. It is, in my view, important to quantify it in terms of the magnitude of shortfall in global supplies that we are facing, which is amplified in time, and the demand destruction event that it is likely to trigger. I intend to incrementally reshape my portfolio in a way that reflects the correlation between the length of the war and the impact it has on the market.

The EIA's Baffling Inventory Build Forecast for This Year

As I have been highlighting since 2024, institutional guidance for the global oil markets coming from the likes of the EIA, IEA & OPEC seems to be increasingly lacking in objectivity, with more and more potential bias built in. My last article on the subject highlighted the discrepancy between the EIA's claim that we had a roughly 4 mb/d global supply surplus in Q4, 2025, versus its own data on global monthly oil production. I concluded that a 2 mb/d surplus was a more likely oil market balance situation. Given reports of massive volumes of output being shut in as the Strait of Hormuz remains blocked, that difference of about 2 mb/d between the EIA's guidance and the more likely reality becomes increasingly important.

While past estimates & forecasts were puzzling, the latest report released on March 10 left me with no doubt that providing as accurate guidance as possible to the global oil & energy markets is no longer the driving force behind the work of such institutions.

As we can see, the EIA still expects a slight 1 mb/d inventory build for the current quarter. Given what was already known about the volumes of oil being shut in by OPEC members in response to not being able to have it transported through the Strait, this forecast is impossible to justify, even if we were to assume that the war will end by the middle of March.

It is thought that by March 9, oil output had been slashed by 2.9 mb/d in Iraq, 2 to 2.5 mb/d in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait slashed production by about 500k b/d, and the UAE saw a decline of 500-800k b/d. We can also assume that Iran's output has declined by more or less the equivalent of its export volumes of 1.5 mb/d. The maximum known cuts in output known to the EIA before the report was completed on the 9th of March were about 7-8 mb/d. Even after the war ends and the Strait of Hormuz is fully open, it will take a few months to fully restore all that lost output. It is therefore mathematically impossible for the world to see any inventory build in the current quarter.

A Billion Barrels of Oil Supply Will Be Lost by Summer

In the first week of conflict, probably about 20-30 million barrels were shut in, mostly in Iran & Iraq. This week, it looks like about 50 million barrels, as storage facilities in the Gulf are filling up fast. If the war does not end, we might be on a path toward a daily supply shortfall of as much as 10-12 mb/d, or about 70-80 million barrels/week, as more oil is shut in.

Some oil seems to be getting through, with Saudi Arabia & UAE diverting it to pipelines that avoid the Strait. Iran also has a pipeline that bypasses the Strait, but it is unclear whether it is making use of it. It is also reported that Iran is still shipping some oil to China via the Strait.

Taking all factors into consideration, we can assume that starting next week, we will lose 75 million barrels/week. Therefore, hypothetically, if this war and blockade of the Strait lasts another 10 weeks, we will lose about 750 million barrels, in addition to the roughly 80 million barrels that we lost in the first 2 weeks. Even once the war ends and the Strait is opened, it will probably take about two months for production to be fully restored. During that time, we can assume we will lose another 200 million barrels or so, bringing the total to roughly 1 billion barrels of oil.

I should note that my estimate of probable volumes of output set to be lost in the event that the war lasts a total of 12 weeks is most likely on the conservative side.

In a worst-case scenario, we could potentially start losing close to 20 mb/d in output, which is nearly double my estimate.

Why We Should Expect a Demand Destruction Event Over the 2026-2027 Period

Based on the EIA's quarterly supply/demand forecasts, we can expect to return to an average supply surplus situation of about 3 mb/d, starting in the second half of this year. What this means is that by the end of 2027, the net supply shortfall of about 1 billion barrels would be largely restored in global inventory levels, without a demand destruction event being necessary to make it happen.

In reality, we are likely to see an average decline of about 1 mb/d in global oil consumption this year and next, and that is how we will partially restore global inventories to pre-war levels by 2027. In practice, this means zero growth in global oil consumption this year and next. The mechanism that will make the market rebalancing happen is most likely to be higher oil prices.

The impact on the global economy will be felt in the form of much lower economic growth. I have been using a formula for over a decade that I have not cited for some time now, which illustrates the oil supply to global economic growth relationship. The formula is based on observing long-term correlations, and it goes as follows.

Global GDP growth = 1.5% (efficiency gains) + (oil output or consumption growth x 2)

Entering zero growth in global oil consumption for this year and next into the equation, we are left with 1.5% global GDP growth on average for this year and next.

One could calculate the effect on the next 4 years instead of 2 by spreading out the supply deficit over a longer period, which would allow us to enter .5% oil consumption growth/year. In that case, we would have 2.5% average economic growth for the next four years.

The IMF had global economic growth averaging about 3.3% for these two years; therefore, we can assume that a 12-week war will shave off about 1.8% from global GDP this year & next.

The impact may not be uniform across all the regions represented in the IMF chart. The euro area, for instance, may be particularly hard-hit since it imports most of its oil, and it is also facing a shortfall in natural gas imports because Qatar's LNG is not flowing, and it has shut in production. The oil price differential between America's WTI and Europe's Brent may also widen due to the continent's overexposure to imports. Japan & S. Korea are faced with similar considerations.

The IEA Decision to Release 400 Million Barrels of Oil

As I started writing this article, the IEA just had its members agree to release 400 million barrels from their strategic reserves. I want to point out that this release does not resolve the problem or have much of an impact on the overall demand destruction event we are likely to experience. The total inventory decline of roughly 1 billion barrels is still set to happen; it is just that it will temporarily alleviate the effects on commercial inventories.

The net effect is that it shifts the timeline. In other words, the effect on oil prices will be less severe in the next few months, while the higher prices might stick around for much longer. This is the preferred policy strategy that I alluded to earlier, where total demand destruction will still amount to about 1 billion barrels, but perhaps it will last for the rest of the decade, rather than happen more immediately.

Expected Effects on Oil Prices

My first instinct in trying to assess the probable path of oil prices was to look back at the 2007-2008 oil price spike as a model. After further reflections and keeping in mind policy preference factors, I believe it will be somewhat different from that event.

In the run-up to the 2008 oil price spike, which established a record high for the WTI benchmark, there were two defining factors that created that massive spike in oil prices, followed by an even deeper decline. The oil price spike ran up against central banks that focused on taming inflation resulting from those higher oil prices.

I do not believe that we will see interest rates pushed higher in an effort to fight off the inflationary pressures resulting from higher oil prices this time around. Neither is the economy afflicted with the systemic risks we had back then in the form of the subprime mortgage crisis. The consumer and business are, therefore, arguably in a more resilient position

The other interesting differentiating factor is that even though oil prices were steadily rising, culminating in that massive spike, SPR reserves were never released.

As we can see, SPR inventory levels were more or less steady, even as oil prices were spiking. With the IEA decision, we can expect perhaps half of the 400 million barrels to be released to come from the US. The SPR is set to be significantly depleted within the next few months. It would not surprise me if further drawdown decisions are made within two months or so if the war continues until then.

What we should expect from oil prices for the rest of the duration of this war is a steady upward march. We may or may not see the 2008 record high price of almost $150/barrel, but whatever price level we will ultimately reach, we are likely to see that price level persist for the duration of the war.

After the war ends, we are likely to see a few months of volatility, with prices mostly headed down. Once global oil output is fully restored, we will most likely see bidding to fill inventories from both commercial entities and governments, which will lead to a higher price floor relative to the pre-war period.

Investment Implications

My initial reaction to the conflict was to prepare for a sharp oil price spike, which is a great opportunity to take profits on my ample oil industry stock positions. At the same time, I was expecting an equally sharp broader market selloff to provide ample opportunities to pick up certain stocks at a bargain. I was expecting a sharp selloff in oil once the war ends and a sharp rebound in stock markets.

I no longer see this playing out as predictably as I initially thought. I still intend to wait until week three of this war before trading, just as I announced in an article I wrote over a week ago. My approach will be even more measured and cautious, making only small changes to my portfolio each week going forward.

My core oil & gas positions consist of Suncor (SU), CNQ (CNQ), and Equinor (EQNR). I have smaller positions in Occidental (OXY), Cheniere (LNG), Petroleo Brasiliero (PBR), and EOG (EOG). My non-core positions will be the first to be reduced or completely eliminated, after which I intend to gradually start reducing my core positions. The latter I intend to keep as a sizable portion of my overall portfolio throughout, for the possibility that this war will not end this year as expected. Once oil prices fall significantly in the post-war period, I intend to start buying again.

As far as bargain hunting, I recently announced my intention to look for a good entry position for SQM (SQM) as well as Mosaic (MOS). However, I mostly intend to look for opportunities throughout the year, possibly targeting dozens of different stocks, which is a big difference from my initial assumption that there will be a narrow window of opportunity to pick up bargains.

A word on gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and silver, which make up a sizable portion of my stock portfolio and are arguably performing poorly relative to expectations as this war unfolds. I expect gold to continue trading in the $4,500 to $5,500 range until the markets come to realize that central banks, including the Federal Reserve, will not raise rates in response to inflationary pressures. At that point, we can expect the next leg up for gold prices to take shape, with silver likely to tag along to some degree.

Risks to My Thesis

This is an ongoing event; therefore, the outcome may still break in many ways. For instance, I am penciling in a total of 12 weeks of conflict, but the war may end suddenly or last for far longer than most people might expect. There is also a risk that vital oil & gas facilities in the region will be damaged, which means that ramping up production & exports again will probably take far longer than expected. There is also a risk that the markets will react in unexpected ways. I expected the market to be erratic in the first two weeks of this conflict, which is why I decided to largely hunker down and hold my current portfolio position. While I expect more predictable market moves beyond the first few weeks, I could be wrong.

Conclusions

Once in a while, we get events that require a complete overhaul of one's portfolio strategy. Since the start of the second Trump administration, we have had two such events. The tariff tantrum from a year ago, which turned out to be a great buying opportunity, was the first, and now the Iran war. What investors do in response to this event will likely shape their portfolio outcome for the rest of this year & next. The longer it continues, the more profound the effect will be, which is why I am adopting an incremental strategy that ramps up the changes to my portfolio based on how long the conflict lasts. I started with my initial response of doing nothing, largely for the first two weeks. Starting next week, I intend to slowly start taking profits on my ample oil positions and start looking at initial bargains. I intend to increase the magnitude with every week that passes, while continuing to assess the situation as time goes by.

data.title: Dollar General: A Big Winner In A Weak Consumer Environment Despite Recent Sell-Off
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Dollar General (DG), I highlighted their attractive valuation and significant expansion, proving their resilience and ability to grow in such a distressed consumer environment while others are struggling.

Despite falling following their latest earnings report, DG is advancing on their debt repayments, improving their fundamentals while advancing on their significant expansion domestically and in Mexico, potentially getting back to significant buybacks soon.

Internal Developments

DG reported a strong Q4 and 2025 as a whole, beating the market’s top- and bottom-line estimates by quite a bit, with cash flow from operations growing by a whopping ~21.3% YoY, with the free cash flow reaching $2.39 billion in 2025, which is ~41.93% higher than 2024’s ~$1.69 billion - all while also reducing their debt significantly.

The stock fell however as a result of the guidance, despite it not being that bad, with DG expecting a 3.7% to 4.2% increase in net sales and 2.2% to 2.7% growth in same-store sales, while the EPS would reach between $7.10 to $7.35, which takes into account a ~$0.13 hit from the expiration of the Work Opportunity Tax Credit at the end of 2025, representing a 3.65% to 7.30% increase YoY, which is not bad compared to the $6.85 seen in 2025, although that was a 34.1% increase compared to the $5.11 seen in 2024.

Meanwhile, CAPEX is expected to reach $1.4 billion to $1.5 billion, a significant jump from their $1.24 billion in 2025, as DG reiterated its plans to execute ~4730 real estate projects this year, which includes ~450 store openings in the US and ~10 in Mexico, while the long-term targets expect 3.5% to 4% net sales growth alongside a ~10% EPS growth rate, ~2% new unit growth, and CAPEX of around 3% of their net sales.

Financially, based on DG’s latest report, we continue to see a strong position, with current assets covering their current liabilities, while the long-term debt has been reduced significantly, for which they halted the buybacks previously.

I'll highlight once again that this move was questionable (not the debt repayment). Looking at it from a longer-term perspective, given the fact that they wasted so much money on buybacks back in 2021-2023 at all-time highs, which would've been more than enough to repay their debt and also build a fortress balance sheet.

Still, now that their leverage is back to a much more manageable level, I wouldn’t be surprised to see DG reintroduce their buybacks alongside the ~1.6% dividend they come at today ($519.5 million per year), potentially seeing over $1 billion in buybacks relatively soon, adding up to a combined yield of >4.75%, which is not terrible, as it would also allow them to continue repaying a good chunk of debt every year or ramp up their investments even more.

Macro Developments

Probably the most important macro development to watch is the ongoing Iran conflict, with oil prices recently jumping again following Iran’s recent attack on oil tankers, causing Iraq to halt operations at all their terminals and lifting the tension in the region.

Again, keep in mind that this is far more than just a local issue, as we’re talking about a country that’s currently blocking the Strait of Hormuz, which can have significant implications for oil, gas, fertilizers, and more, which can already be seen in higher pump prices.

At the same time, shipping companies like Maersk (AMKBY) even announced that they’re not going to take the risk and instead avoid even the Suez Canal, preferring to take the long Cape of Good Hope route around Africa, which can add even more pressure due to higher global shipping costs, delays, or even supply chain disruptions, which can cause significant inflation - or worse.

Not only that, but this comes at a terrible time for the global economy, while US consumer weakness has been reported for years at this point in virtually every sector, with recent consumer sentiment data showing the weakest levels since 2014 not long ago, before improving recently. This can even amplify the ongoing trade-downs, although that’s not as bad for a company like DG, which is able to win a lot from the advancing K-shaped economic discrepancy. This way, it’s no wonder that DG is expanding aggressively and delivering such impressive results.

Not long ago, we also saw some economic improvements and even the Supreme Court’s tariff decision, which can be positive for the economy overall despite the subsequent increase to 15%. Since DG is far from immune to these tariffs (being forced to raise prices not long ago), this could be an improvement overall, despite the company's relatively low direct import exposure.

In this case, we're talking about a double-edged sword, as it can affect them by forcing some price hikes, but it also affects the consumer and potentially puts more pressure on them that actually leads to more store traffic.

Still, private labels are ultimately a big risk for DG, as giants like Walmart (WMT) are able to not only gain significant power of negotiation against legacy brands and get better deals, but they can also gather incredible amounts of data, which could allow them to adapt and tailor their offer to suit the evolving consumer needs, all while being able to deliver great prices.

In fact, I mentioned before how Walmart’s Great Value brand had some products that were even cheaper than a dollar store’s, which is certainly impressive in this economy. Not only does it allow these retailers to capture more of the consumers’ budgets, potentially replacing the need to even go to a dollar store, but it can also boost the trust in their private label, which is something that legacy brands have to pay billions for in the form of marketing.

This way, they can launch more and more products under it, offer different price points, and potentially pose a threat for DG since you know the kind of value you get for your dollar and can trust the brand, so you don’t have to go to a dollar store as often, especially when economic conditions improve, pushing dollar stores into a tough position where they have to explore higher price points or rely on the “treasure hunt” experience to attract customers - or even launching their own private labels.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, since the company is kind of a hedge on the economy, we could expect the market to react negatively to the potential slowdown caused by eventual economic improvements, even if the company can do pretty well in such an environment, as capital would likely flow to better risk-adjusted alternatives.

Meanwhile, longer-term pressure from other retailers and their private label brands can lead to what I called a “loss of identity” before in the dollar store segment, as these companies are ultimately not immune to macro pressure either.

There’s certainly the risk of seeing names like Walmart or especially Aldi steal their spot, which is likely a reason why DG and others focus on smaller, rural cities that may not be viable for a Walmart, although Aldi’s aggressive expansion may still pose a major threat.

Valuation

For DG's DCF to Equity valuation, given the elevated level of CAPEX and the rest of their guidance, I'll assume an FCF of $2.25 billion in 2026, assuming net borrowings remain relatively flat given a much better level of leverage seen now.

I'll assume they can grow this at a relatively modest 6.5% CAGR until year 5, which is actually below their 10% EPS target and closer to the revenue instead, which is not hard given their growth plans and potential ramped-up international expansion, followed by a 4.25% CAGR until year 10, a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and a relatively high 10% discount rate to take into account potential risks and demand a better margin of safety, as well as 221.31 million shares outstanding.

This way, we get to an equity value of $36.05 billion, for an intrinsic value of $162.89 per share, which continues to be above the current $134.22 seen at the moment of writing this, despite using conservative estimates, which the company can easily beat.

Conclusions

DG remains a Buy, backed by an attractive valuation, improving financials, and strong results, with a price that’s now getting even better following the recent drop.

There are certainly risks to consider, especially from the competition, but the valuation continues to offer a good enough margin of safety, making the risk-reward still attractive even when we take that into account.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: IEA Flags Record Oil Supply Disruption Due To U.S.-Israel-Iran Conflict
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

IEA says 7.5% of global oil supply will be disrupted, the most in history. (0:15) Goldman delays expected Fed rate cuts amid inflation risks. (0:54) Eli Lilly warns on compounded weight-loss drug. (1:54)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict has triggered what the International Energy Agency calls the largest supply shock in the history of global oil markets, disrupting roughly 7.5% of worldwide supply.

In its monthly report, the IEA said escalating hostilities following U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran on February 28 have severely curtailed flows through the Strait of Hormuz — one of the world’s most critical energy arteries.

Tanker traffic through the strait, which carried about 20M barrels per day of crude and refined products last year, has fallen more than 90%, according to multiple media reports.

The agency estimates the conflict could reduce global oil supply by around 8M barrels per day this month.

On the economic front, that sound echoing through Wall Street?

Economists erasing their Fed forecasts.

Goldman Sachs has pushed back its expected timing for the Federal Reserve’s first rate cut this year, citing higher inflation risks tied to rising oil prices and geopolitical tensions involving Iran.

Goldman now expects the FOMC to begin easing in September, rather than earlier in the year. The bank still anticipates two cuts in 2026, with the second likely in December.

“But if the labor market weakens sooner and more substantially than we expect, we do not think concern about the impact of higher oil prices on inflation and inflation expectations would be an obstacle to earlier rate cuts,” economists said.

Among active stocks, Blue Owl Capital (OWL) told investors at a recent private conference that its sale of $1.4B in loans from three of its funds included no backstops or hidden incentives, according to Bloomberg.

Blue Owl said the four institutions that bought the debt did so on an arm’s-length basis, conducted their own due diligence, purchased on identical terms, and received no special guarantees.

Eli Lilly (LLY) issued a public warning about potential safety risks tied to compounded versions of the weight-loss drug tirzepatide when mixed with vitamin B12.

Lilly said testing found “significant levels of an impurity” caused by a chemical reaction between the vitamin and tirzepatide — the active ingredient in its blockbuster drug Zepbound.

And Dick’s Sporting Goods (DKS) is higher after beating Q4 estimates, marking its first full quarter incorporating Foot Locker results.

The retailer now sees FY27 revenue of $22.1B to $22.4B, roughly in line with the $22.1B consensus, and EPS of $13.50 to $14.50, below the $14.97 consensus.

In other news of note, artificial intelligence is advancing so quickly that companies should stop thinking of it as just a better chatbot and start treating it as a system that can carry out hours of independent work, according to Ethan Mollick.

Speaking at the UBS Global Consumer and Retail Conference, the Wharton School professor said AI has entered a new phase in which agents can plan, research, write code and complete tasks with limited human input.

That shift, he said, has major implications for white-collar work, corporate strategy and consumer behavior.

“If you haven’t changed anything you do as a result of having these tools — if you haven’t changed any part of the process — that’s a mistake,” Mollick said.

In his view, AI is no longer a novelty or a future concept.

It is already capable enough to reshape how many kinds of business work get done — and companies waiting for perfect clarity may find the disruption arrives first.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Bank of America says Healthcare (XLV) and Information Technology (XLK) – that old defensive/growth combo -- are the favored sectors among stocks with the highest hedge fund exposure.

Looking at S&P names with the most net relative exposure by hedge funds, the top holdings include Incyte (INCY), Centene (CNC), Hologic (HOLX), News Corp. (NWSA) and VeriSign (VRSN).

You can see the full top 20 here.

data.title: Tehran's Economic Trojan Horse: Using High Inflation To Humble The U.S.
data.description:
In the current market landscape, understanding geopolitics is probably the most important skill for an investor. Over the long term, we know that EPS rules the stock market valuation, but over the short-medium term, what is currently happening between Iran and the US/Israel is the main market mover. For this reason, analyzing the development of this conflict is now my main interest: I don't want to be on the wrong side of history.

To avoid any misunderstanding, I want to clarify a couple of things. First of all, I want to believe that this war will end sometime soon and we will forget about it in a couple of weeks, but I can’t for the reasons I am going to show you later. Second, to predict how a conflict will evolve, you must empathize with both parties involved; otherwise, your prediction will end up being excessively biased. If you are biased, you are going to be wrong and ultimately lose money. Saying that Iran shouldn’t be downplayed doesn’t mean being pro-Iran, but it means maintaining a cautious approach against the enemy.

With that being said, let’s dive into my analysis.

Facts And Words Don't Match

The ultimate goal for both Israel and the US is to control Iran and prevent it from having nuclear weapons, and it can only be possible if the government is replaced by someone appointed by President Trump. But to get this done, Iran must lose its existential war.

Frankly, when Ali Khamenei was assassinated, I thought that Iran’s surrender was a matter of hours, but what came later totally surprised me. Days after the assassination of the Supreme Leader, his son Mojtaba Khamenei took command, a clear affront to President Trump’s willingness:

I’m not going through this to end up with another Khamenei. I want to be involved in the selection.

President Trump’s words two days before Mojtaba Khamenei appointment.

Overall, the evidence of 12 days of war shows that both Israel and the US are far from the much-discussed "regime change". However, since this war has to end with a winner who controls the Middle East, I believe that we have just entered the early stages of it: there is still too much uncertainty about who is going to win. On the other hand, my certainty is that either of the parties can't withdraw at this point; otherwise, it would be an admission of having lost the war. In other words, there is further room for escalation.

As long as the market doesn't discount a plausible exit strategy for this war, I believe oil prices will continue to increase. In addition, don't give too much importance to what happened over the last few days. Oil prices dropped about 30% from Monday’s $120 high, and the market believed that the worst of this conflict was already behind us. Well, the same happened in 2022 when Russia invaded Ukraine. Everyone talked about a short-lived war, but we all know how it ended up.

From early March to early August, oil prices have been most of the time above the $100 threshold and even above $110 in some weeks. And that war, in terms of supply chain energy shock, I believe it wasn’t as concerning as the current one. Anyway, getting back to what is happening now, the two major reasons that have caused the price of oil to plummet, in my opinion, won’t invert its bullish trend.

The first one was the decision by the International Energy Agency (members of advanced economies primarily in North America, Europe, and Northeast Asia) to unleash 400 million barrels held in reserves in order to face the current oil supply shock. The International Energy Agency (IEA) holds 1.20 billion oil barrels, and 600 million oil barrels are held by industry stocks, so the entire system can rely on 1.80 billion oil barrels to face a potential oil supply crisis.

At first glance, this number seems very high, and it actually is, but these reserves weren’t designed to bear a prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz: that’s a black swan event. In 2024, on average, oil flow through the strait was 20 million barrels per day, about 1.80 billion in three months and 7.30 billion in one year.

Basically, IEA reserves can only afford a three-month war. After that, governments all over the world need to curb energy consumption in one way or another. Actually, it is already happening in Southeast Asia. I don't think this conflict can last as long as the one in Ukraine, but I think at least 3 months are plausible.

Over the first 12 days, very little has been accomplished in terms of regime change, and that’s the aspect that I consider the most important to understand in which stage of the war we are at. However, to President Trump, the war is in its final stage, and that’s the reason number two why oil prices dropped over the last days:

I think the war is very complete, pretty much. (Iran has) no navy, no communications, they've got no air force. Their missiles are down to a scatter. Their drones are being blown up all over the place, including their manufacturing of drones. If you look, they have nothing left. There's nothing left in a military sense.

To put it simply, Iran has nothing left to retaliate to US/Israel attacks. No navy, no air force—a total disaster that will bring them to accept an unconditional surrender. These statements were made two days ago, so I suppose that the current situation is even worse for Iran.

I want to believe that, but evidence shows me that facts and words don’t match:

  • The Strait of Hormuz is still under Iranian control, and oil tankers are not free to navigate through it. According to Reuters, “The U.S. Navy has refused near-daily requests from the shipping industry for military escorts.” This fact goes against President Trump’s vision that the US can control passage through the Strait. This morning, according to United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO), three cargo ships were hit by projectiles near the Iranian coast.
  • For a country that has nothing left in a military sense, it is rather contradictory that this morning it launched its most intense strikes of the war. Do they have missiles left or not?

Overall, I believe that the markets are downplaying the effect of this war, partly because it has just started (it takes months to suffer the consequences) and partially because the US is already claiming a crushing victory. Since the current Supreme Leader is the son of Khamenei and the Strait of Hormuz is still closed, I honestly wonder what kind of victory they are talking about.

Anyway, let’s assume that the US ‘wins the war’ and wants to withdraw. Is this enough to stop the energy crisis in the Middle East? I have my doubts since the US is not the only actor in this war. The Middle East needs a clear winner between Israel and Iran, a country that will rule that part of the world over the next decades.

Since Israel is much more powerful in terms of military, the market is assuming that a ceasefire would be a sort of victory for Iran, but are we really sure about that?

America and Israel cannot end the war whenever they want. The Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic and the armed forces demand revenge. We will continue the war until the enemy regrets and is defeated.

Iranian Major General Ali Abdollahi

The Iranian Plan: Simple And Effective

Having the best weapons is one of the biggest advantages when you enter a war, but it is not the only condition that will make you win. On paper, the US and Israel combined should have destroyed Iran in a couple of hours, but that didn’t happen to the point of achieving the main goal: regime change. With military aircraft, you can destroy whatever you want, but Iran is a very large country, munitions are not infinite, and you probably need boots on the ground to control the Iranian political system. A very complex operation that you can’t achieve in a couple of weeks.

Iran is a poor country and has zero chances to destroy the US/Israel, but its biggest advantage is that it is aware of its own limits. Iran knows that it can’t rely on very powerful ballistic missiles, let alone a Northrop Grumman B-2 Spirit. For these reasons, they are not following the same strategy as the US/Israel. While the latter are relying on shock and awe, Iran aims at something else: destroying the oil supply chain, consequently generating a recession in most developed countries. Iran doesn’t want to kill Western leaders; it wants to kill our economic system. Without money, the US can no longer afford to fight the war:

U.S. officials are posting fake news to manipulate markets. It won't protect them from inflationary tsunami they've imposed on Americans.

Markets are facing biggest shortfall in HISTORY: bigger than Arab Oil Embargo, Iran's Islamic Revolution and the Kuwait invasion COMBINED.

Seyed Abbas Araghchi, Foreign Minister Islamic Republic of Iran

To put it simply, in this war Iran will mainly focus on ways to destroy the US (and its allies) economy, and blocking the Strait of Hormuz is the ultimate goal (up to now, it has been achieved). Destroying oil/gas infrastructures of the Gulf countries is a way to keep the oil supply emergency active even after the war ends (it needs time to repair them). Since oil prices are strongly correlated to Core CPI, Iran wants to knock out the world economy by using high inflation as a weapon. At that point, central banks might be forced to increase interest rates, and we all know that most developed economies can’t afford that due to their huge debt burden.

In light of these considerations, what we can notice is that Iran’s plan seems much simpler and more effective compared to the US/Israel’s. The latter wants to overturn the Iranian regime, but how do they even plan something like this? There are too many variables for such a huge country with a population of 90 million, and that, apparently, doesn’t seem impressed by the recent attacks.

Iran doesn't fear your empty threats. Even those bigger than you couldn't eliminate Iran. Be careful not to get eliminated yourself.

Ali Larijani, the head of the Iranian National Security Council

Ali Larijani might have just said what was expected of him, but the fact that the US/Israel plan seems quite complex to implement remains (especially without ground troops). It is possible, but it requires a huge effort.

On the other hand, Iran knows exactly how to follow its plan, and it doesn’t need high-tech military to achieve it. All it needs is to just fire some missiles/drones against tankers in the Strait, and ship insurers will stay away from this route. Without insurance, it is not convenient for tankers to cross the Strait. A low-budget plan, but evidence tells us that it is working. Oil prices are still pretty high compared to 27 February. The EIA unleashed 400 million barrels to bear this oil supply shock (almost double compared to 2022), and we are not even in the third week of the conflict. Imagine if this chaos lasted 3-4 months.

We won’t allow even one liter of oil to reach the US, Zionists and their partners. Any vessel or tanker bound to them will be a legitimate target. Get ready for the oil barrel to be at $200 because the oil price depends on the regional security which you have destabilized.

Ebrahim Zolfaqari, spokesperson for the Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters.

To sum everything up, even though inferior from a military point of view, the Iranian strategy is simpler to achieve, and I believe this is a key aspect for the future developments of this war.

What Am I Actually Doing?

Sooner or later, Israel would have attacked Iran to expand its influence across the Middle East and eliminate its biggest threat; the US couldn’t be outside this war. Experts already expected this, but what is surprising is Iran’s reaction, in my opinion. The Iranian government seems really committed to destroying the energy supply chain, regardless of the constant bombing from the US/Israel.

From a purely economic point of view, I see this war as a significant catalyst that will make the inflation rate a problem once again. Therefore, I am staying out of these investments:

  • Long-term bonds. If the inflation rate ramps up, central banks must increase interest rates, and bond prices will go down. Furthermore, since most developed countries are suffocated by a huge debt, I don’t feel that confident that in 20-30 years I will get my money back. Even if I get it, the real returns could be negative if we face a new inflationary wave.
  • Speculative stocks with unstable cash flows and totally dependent on the AI narrative. Triple-digit PE ratios are no longer allowed, so I would avoid companies like Palantir.
  • All cryptocurrencies, as I don’t expect them to be a hedge against inflation. In fact, 2022 was a very negative year for them.

What I am actively doing:

  • I am switching my EUR to USD as I expect the appreciation of the latter will continue. The Eurozone is much more sensitive to the energy supply destruction (I wrote an article about it); therefore, it will suffer the worst outcome from this conflict. If I were American, I would consider the exchange rate risk of investing in a European company right now.
  • I have increased my liquidity a lot and invested in EUR/USD ETFs that include short-term bonds. I want to seize the future opportunities that this conflict will create.
  • Of course, I didn’t stop my accumulation plan because of this war. I keep buying a predetermined monthly quantity of an ETF that tracks the global stock market. This is my future pension.
  • I keep looking for selective buying opportunities. I have recently bought some shares of Netflix, Booking, S&P Global, Visa, Uber, and Ferrari. All these businesses present high pricing power, and that’s a crucial aspect to take into account during inflationary phases. Plus, their valuations seem cheap right now.
  • After the massive 30% drop, I bought a European ETC that tracks WTI oil prices. It is a very small position, though, compared to the rest of the portfolio, so it won’t totally offset the potential losses if the Core CPI gets to 4-5%. I don’t want a total hedge, as I prioritize my long-term strategy compared to short- and medium-term trades.

So, as you can see, even though I don’t have rosy expectations about the future of the economy, this is not preventing me from buying some stocks. I have a rather high confidence that the war won’t end anytime soon, but waiting for the end of the world before buying doesn’t make sense. It is okay to be cautious, but being too pessimistic usually doesn't pay off in the stock market.

data.title: goeasy: Too Much Uncertainty To Own Right Now
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Note: This is an update to my previous article.

goeasy Ltd. (GSY:CA) (EHMEF) was considered for a long time a lending business with a secret formula that allowed it to maintain a high-quality business, benefiting both the company and the subprime clients. However, the stock recently plummeted 65% after the company revealed credit deterioration and aggressive accounting practices in the past, forcing new management to recognize large bad debt losses, withdraw its guidance, and suspend dividends and share buybacks. While the new management team appears to be taking decisive steps to address the problem, the current situation seems more serious than a simple temporary issue. Given the uncertainty surrounding the loan portfolio and the time it could take to regain investor confidence, I recommend staying on the sidelines for now.

The Financial Update

goeasy has plummeted 65% in two days following a very bad Financial Update released on March 10th. If you had bought the stock at any point in the last five years, you would be sitting at a loss today. So, what caused this plunge?

First, this update suggests a significant deterioration in its LendCare segment, where the company lends money and earns interest. Specifically, goeasy expects to record an additional $178 million in charge-offs, bringing the total amount of charge-offs for the quarter to approximately $331 million. In simple terms, this means the company has concluded that a large portion of those loans will likely never be repaid, so it must take the loss. This is negative because it immediately reduces net profits and also may raise concerns about the quality of the loans, which may have been significantly lower than previously thought.

Secondly, the company also announced it will increase its loan loss provision by approximately $86 million, indicating it expects its loan portfolio to perform worse than anticipated. To mitigate these defaults, the company must set aside additional funds to absorb these potential losses, which suggests the problem may not be temporary. This also explains why goeasy withdrew its previously issued 3-year guidance.

What surprised me the most was that the company also announced that it will review certain historical reporting practices within the LendCare business, specifically related to how some customer payments were recorded. These payments had previously been recognized as received while still in the settlement process, and in some cases were ultimately never collected. This was probably the most damaging part of the announcement. For a lender, trust in the accuracy of credit metrics is critical, and if those numbers are questioned, investors naturally begin to wonder whether the loan portfolio was actually performing as well as previously thought or if aggressive accounting practices played a role.

At that point, many investors likely lost confidence in the company, but then management also decided to suspend the dividend and stopped share buybacks in order to preserve cash and strengthen the balance sheet while the company works through the current credit issues. While this decision makes financial sense, it also removes two of the main reasons many investors owned the stock in the first place. Without the dividend and buybacks, and with uncertainty around the business, the sell-off accelerated, and years of stock gains disappeared in just 2 days.

A Potential Turnaround Story?

Something that caught my attention is the high turnover of executives the company has experienced recently. Between 2024 and 2026, the company has already had three different CEOs: Jason Mullins in 2024, Dan Rees, who resigned in December 2025 and was replaced by Patrick Ens in January 2026. Also, the CFO resigned too in September 2025, leaving Felix Wu in his position. So many changes and resignations lead me to believe that problems were already brewing internally long before investors became aware of them.

What I find interesting about the recent financial update is that the new management team seems to be handling the situation very differently. Instead of trying to smooth things over or postpone the inevitable, they decided to take the hit all at once. Reporting the additional charge-offs, withdrawing the previous guidance, and suspending the dividend were obviously very painful for shareholders, but it also suggests the new leadership team is trying to clean up the situation rather than pushing the problem further into the future. As the CFO said, “We are taking definitive action to rectify this situation, and we recognize that LendCare's recent rapid growth ... enhanced credit risk management practices."

To address the situation, the company introduced a 6-point action plan aimed at stabilizing the business. Part of the strategy is to refocus growth on easyfinancial’s core lending business, which historically has been more predictable. At the same time, management plans to reduce auto and powersports loan originations through LendCare’s merchant channels, which appear to be where most of the credit problems originated. From my perspective, this suggests the new management team understands where things went wrong and is trying to correct those weaknesses going forward.

The Bottom Line

In my previous coverage, I assigned a hold rating to the company because it wasn't the type of business I typically invest in and the macroeconomic environment made it a less attractive business at the time. I honestly didn’t expect a situation like this to happen less than 3 months after my writing.

For now, it seems that management is taking the right steps to confront the problem, but this is not the kind of situation where I'd feel comfortable assigning a buy rating given the level of uncertainty surrounding the business. Remember, this isn't a situation of a bad quarter or slightly worse than expected guidance. It's literally a business restructuring due to aggressive accounting practices that were masking the reality. Until the company proves that the loan portfolio is under control and that these issues are truly behind it, I believe it is better to stay on the sidelines.

The fund seeks capital appreciation.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • US equities advanced in the fourth quarter despite renewed volatility amid economic crosscurrents and seemingly mixed investor sentiment toward artificial intelligence (AI) valuations.
  • The US government shutdown appeared to weigh on confidence as key economic data releases were delayed. Once released, the initial third quarter GDP estimate showed robust 4.3% growth.
  • Labor market conditions softened, as November’s jobs report showed weaker non-farm payrolls and unemployment rose to 4.6%, a four-year high. Consumer spending remained resilient but showed strain among lower income households.
  • Inflation moderated but November’s 2.7% headline CPI remained above the Fed’s 2% target.
  • The Fed cut the federal funds rate twice during the quarter, signaling a cautious shift toward easing while emphasizing its commitment to balance inflation pressures with growing labor market risks.
  • Despite the mid-quarter volatility spike, strong corporate earnings and Fed rate cuts fueled equity gains. The Russell Midcap Index returned 0.16% while the S&P 500 Index returned 2.66%.
  • Regardless of market sentiment and near-term economic trends, our investment process favors better-managed companies with strong balance sheets and competitive positioning. We seek to outperform through stock selection while minimizing any top-down macro, factor and sector exposures relative to the index.

Portfolio positioning

We maintain our valuation discipline and our focus on companies with competitive advantages and skilled management teams that we believe are executing better than their peers. These companies historically tend to have higher profit margins and returns on invested capital, rising market shares and consistently strong pricing power. As of quarter end, all sector weights were within +/- 3% of the Russell Midcap Index.

The largest additions to the fund during the quarter included the following:

Take-Two Interactive Software (TTWO) is a leading video game developer and publisher that we believe offers an attractive risk/reward profile, supported by its strong franchises and growth potential in interactive entertainment.

Tractor Supply (TSCO) is a leading retailer of rural and lifestyle products. We initiated a position after recent share weakness created a compelling entry point, in our view.

Carlyle Group (CG) is a leading alternative investment manager that has delivered strong results under CEO Harvey Schwartz; we believe the stock remains undervalued relative to industry peers.

Datadog (DDOG) is a leading provider of cloud monitoring and security solutions that we believe is positioned for above-average growth as enterprises migrate workloads to the cloud.

Yum! Brands (YUM) has outperformed peers in same-store sales at Taco Bell and KFC; we see additional upside as management has prioritized its strongest brands while reviewing strategic options for its lagging Pizza Hut segment.

Positions sold during the quarter included the following:

Tyler Technologies (TYL) was sold because the software provider’s revenue growth has decelerated, which reduced our conviction in its upside potential.

Blue Owl Capital (OWL), an alternatives investment manager, was sold in favor of businesses that have more diversified exposure beyond private credit.

Ascendis Pharma (ASND) was sold because increasing regulatory and competitive headwinds reduced our conviction in the biotech company’s growth prospects.

Tapestry (TPR), which owns luxury brands like Coach and Kate Spade, was sold after its share price rallied in 2025, bringing it close to our upside target.

CACI International (CACI) was sold as its strong performance made its forward risk/reward profile less compelling to us.

HealthEquity (HQY), which manages health savings accounts (HSAs), was sold because a lower interest rate environment may put pressure on the significant interest revenue it historically earns from HSA cash balances.

The fund’s Class A shares at net asset value (NAV) returned 0.60% for the quarter, outperforming the Russell Midcap Index, which returned 0.16%. Contributors to performance

MongoDB posted robust results, with total revenue exceeding expectations and growth in cloud revenue accelerating. We maintain a constructive view of the company following its recent stock rally, supported by our confidence in the newly appointed CEO’s ability to scale the business and deliver profitable growth.

ATI outperformed as earnings exceeded expectations and management raised full-year earnings-per-share (EPS) guidance above consensus. Strong demand for jet engines – a key earnings driver – provides visibility into 2027, while the stock has been trading at a discount to larger peer Howmet (not a fund holding), a gap we believe should narrow over time.

Guardant Health reported strong quarterly results, driven by sales momentum across its diagnostics solutions and aided by its strategic partnerships with leading testing centers. Management reported that its core business segments turned cash flow positive earlier than expected and signaled to us continued strength by raising its forward revenue guidance.

Detractors from performance

BellRing Brands, which sells Premier Protein products, has faced rising competitive headwinds. Increased distribution of Coca-Cola’s Fairlife brand and the entrance of other market players have pressured BellRing’s market share, creating in our view potential challenges for the company’s future growth trajectory.

Bullish is a globally focused institutional cryptocurrency exchange. While we maintain a constructive view of the company’s fundamentals, its share price underperformance seems to have been driven by broader investor sentiment tied to recent weakness in cryptocurrencies.

Royal Caribbean underperformed as cruise stocks retreated from summer highs. Its stock lagged following management guidance that indicated negative impacts from one-time costs and expected tax headwinds. We believe the company remains better positioned than peers, supported by solid customer demand and a best-in-class balance sheet that should allow it to deliver new ships and take market share from competitors.

data.title: Student Living EduVation Financials Sag As It Launches U.S. IPO
data.description:
Student Living EduVation’s Growth Rate And Margins Are Dropping

Student Living EduVation (Holdings) Corporation (SDLV) has filed proposed terms for a $19 million U.S. IPO, according to an amended F-1/A registration statement.

The company provides rental housing options to students in the greater Hong Kong area.

Its topline revenue growth rate has dropped sharply, despite a small base, and its major margin metrics are also falling quickly.

The firm is thinly capitalized, faces intense competition, and is subject to a wide variety of market threats, from fragmentation to government regulatory policy risks.

The SDLV IPO appears grossly overvalued, so my outlook on it is to Avoid it.

What Does Student Living Do?

The company provides housing solutions to domestic and international students studying at various educational institutions in Hong Kong.

It generates most of its revenue from sub-leasing properties it leases, as the revenue contribution pie chart shows here:

Education Advisory services (producing referral fees to SDLV) are provided to students via a related party partner, Meta Academia, so the company has material revenues from a related party and is subject to concentration risk in this regard.

The firm also has a limited number of suppliers, producing concentration risks in the process.

SDLV leases residential properties near universities in Hong Kong, converts them into student dormitories and provides ongoing cleaning, maintenance and security services.

The company Chairman and CEO of the company is Mr. Chun Kwok Ng, who has been with the firm since 2022 and has a financial background in the education consulting industry and the accounting industry.

What is Student Living’s Market?

The company's market is currently limited to Hong Kong and is exhibiting limited supply, forcing students to expend considerable time, effort and money to secure their own lodgings.

The market is seeing a growing number of students coming in from mainland China and other regions, and Student Living provides an integrated set of services to help these students.

The government’s initiatives to enhance the city’s reputation for drawing students have led to increased investment and a positive overall regulatory environment in Hong Kong, according to a 2024 market report by Cushman & Wakefield.

However, a shortage of student beds was expected to worsen in the coming years due to insufficient hostel capacity, with one forecast showing a supply shortfall of 47,000 in recent years.

SDLV faces competition from agencies, individual room landlords, hostels, educational referral providers and other alternative accommodation providers.

Recent industry trends include a greater degree of professionalization of the industry as investors look at student housing as a more predictable and stable real estate asset class, integrated service offerings and catering to mainland China students.

However, the industry faces challenges since it relies heavily on student visa policies by the government, college admission quotas and other education policies that impact the supply and demand of students for education in Hong Kong.

SDLV’s strengths include its integrated business model, property locations near colleges and comprehensive support services and social/cultural activities to improve student retention.

Its weak points are that it relies almost exclusively on leased properties for dormitory supply, exposing the company to lease renewal and rent increase risks, and its operations are limited to Hong Kong, resulting in geographic concentration.

Opportunities on tap for SDLV include expanding its dorm portfolio, both within the greater Hong Kong region and in Malaysia, and continuing and developing partnerships for more housing supply and educational advisory providers.

The company is exposed to the potential of student declines due to government or college policy changes; it is also subject to changing macroeconomic conditions and intense competition from alternative lodging providers of many types.

Student Living’s Recent Financial Results

The company's recent financial results are shown in the table below.

From a small base, revenue has grown quickly but at a decelerating rate of growth, while gross margin, operating margin and net margin have all fallen sharply in the most recent half-year reporting period.

Student Living’s IPO Plan

SDLV has filed to sell 3.75 million shares of common stock at a propose midpoint price of $5.00 per share, for gross proceeds of $18.75 million, excluding customary underwriter over allotments.

The lock-up period for the company, shareholders and employees will be for 180 days after the closing of the IPO, which typically occurs several days after initial open market trading.

Management is seeking an enterprise value of $105 million, a chart float ratio of 15.8% and net tangible book value dilution to new shareholders of $3.41.

SDLV plans to use the net proceeds from the IPO as follows:

We currently intend to use the net proceeds from this Offering for [i] the Group’s Hong Kong business development; [ii] building the Group’s presence in the Southeast Asian market; [iii] developing our student residence and dormitory management system; and [v] for working capital and general corporate purposes.

(Source - SEC.)

The firm’s roadshow is not available for online viewing.

Management said the company is not a party to any legal actions that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or operations.

The only listed underwriter of the IPO is D. Boral Capital.
