Student Living’s Growth Rate And Margins Are Falling

Student Living seeks to obtain capital U.S. public capital market investors, despite a rapidly slowing revenue growth rate and margin compression across major metrics.

Many Asian companies have sought to raise the new Nasdaq minimum IPO amount of $15 million, but so far in 2026, there have been no successful Asian company IPOs.

I’m not surprised by this, as their generally poor performance in previous years has effectively "burned" their investor base in the U.S., so now that the Nasdaq has rightfully increased the minimum amount, they have been unable to close transactions.

The firm’s growth strategy includes expanding its dormitory network through developer collaborations, providing short-term rental options and entering new markets like Malaysia.

SDLV has already planned a 1,000-student dormitory project in Malaysia.

The market for student housing in Hong Kong is large and faces a significant ongoing shortage of beds for the foreseeable future, so Student Living has positive industry growth dynamics in its favor.

The company is a "foreign private issuer" and "emerging growth company," which means management can provide substantially less information to shareholders.

Many of these types of companies, especially Asian firms, have performed poorly or even been delisted from U.S. stock exchanges after their IPO.

Also, trade relations between China and the U.S. have been volatile in recent years, so the company's ability to continue to be a listed U.S. stock is subject to potentially rapid regulatory changes from either China or the U.S.

As to valuation, management is asking public IPO investors to pay an EV/Revenue multiple of 34x and an EV/EBITDA multiple of 57x, both of which appear excessive.

Given the highly speculative nature of the company, its thin capitalization, sharply falling growth rate and margin compression, and numerous risks to investors, my outlook on the SDLV IPO is to Sell (avoid it).

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Tourmaline Oil: Deep Value Remains As Major Inflection Point Approaches
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Tourmaline Oil (TRMLF) (TOU:CA), I stated that “There's Deep Value In Canada's Top Natural Gas Producer,” as Tourmaline continued to prove their resilience, disciplined capital allocation and significant strategic expansion, allowing them to capture the industry’s long-term potential.

With a solid report released recently and the company advancing on their long-term plans, Tourmaline remains very attractive, standing to benefit from significant macro tailwinds that can benefit the company, its country and the commodities they produce.

Internal Developments

Tourmaline reported a solid 2025 overall, continuing their best-in-class capital efficiencies and growing their natural gas production significantly alongside the rest of the business, with relatively stable cash flow from operations and a significant ramp-up in CAPEX for their projects, all while holding ~75 years' worth of drilling inventory.

As mentioned, the company expects the following couple of years to continue delivering relatively weak free cash flow, as most of the cash from operations would go into developing their projects, expecting a significant jump from 2028 thanks to the NEBC Montney gas/condensate complex and the premium international sales with strong margins.

They also announced the sale of their Peace River High asset to Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) for net proceeds of C$765 million, helping them reduce net debt to about C$1.5 billion, as they say during their Q4 Earnings Call. I like both of these companies and I’ll reiterate that Tourmaline arguably has some of the best management in oil and gas on the planet, and I’m sure they wouldn’t just sell these for no reason.

Especially since they’re exposed so much to AECO prices, reducing their net debt by such a big chunk is not bad in this environment, improving their resilience and potentially cleaning the balance sheet while making room for future purchases.

Financially, based on Tourmaline’s latest report, we continue to see a solid position, with an overall manageable amount of leverage, especially after the asset sale mentioned above, which is not visible in this report since that was completed in February.

Again, given the drop in FCF, I really wouldn’t expect some massive dividends other than the base - which remains sustainable on top of the maintenance CAPEX with NYMEX/AECO at $1.50/Mcf (assuming no growth CAPEX), while the rest until ~$3.50/Mcf goes into the significant growth projects they’re working on, and the “excess” goes into additional returns and investments, although their excess FCF would grow significantly from 2028 onwards.

Sure, the current base dividend is not sustainable at current prices, but the company’s expected growth should more than cover it in the future, and the recent asset sales should help them stabilize and buy enough time until the growth projects are completed.

Macro Developments

Since we’re talking about commodities that depend significantly on international markets, the ongoing Iran conflict will be essential for Tourmaline as well, especially since we’re talking about a region that plays a key role not only for oil, but also natural gas, with the initial strikes against Qatar for example causing a ~50% jump in one day in European gas on the Monday following the attack.

Again, this remains a matter of time and will ultimately depend on the goal here, while I’ll reiterate that Iran is a massive (armed) natural fortress, and that factor combined with several other logistic, natural and especially religious aspects would make a regime change nearly impossible and certainly way too costly from any point of view (especially timewise) - with American troops on the ground being quite mandatory.

Not only would the risk of failure be significant, but it can also trigger a wider conflict in the region, putting even more pressure on these commodities - and more - which is why I expect (and hope, from a humanitarian point of view) that this is just a “quick skirmish,” which so far seems to be the case given Trump’s recent statements where he said that this may end “pretty quickly.”

Still, until this is over and while the Strait of Hormuz stays blocked (which Iran stated will be the case for as long as the attacks persist), we’re very likely to see these effects ripple and scale throughout the global economy - even if oil were to drop to ~$84 at the moment of writing this as G7 states are working on a coordinated release of oil from reserves.

These commodities are ultimately vital for the global economy from much more than just the direct uses of oil and gas, with >90% of today's sulfur (which is key for sulfuric acid manufacturing) for example being recovered from oil and gas, which is key for fertilizers, metal processing and extraction (for several commodities including copper, nickel, zinc, etc.), which by itself can have significant effects on the broader economy, and that’s just one “simple” branch of a massive global supply chain. And that’s on top of shipping companies avoiding even the Suez Canal due to the critical level of risks, potentially leading to significant supply chain disruptions, inflation, and more.

Sure, the longer these high prices and “war premiums” last for, the better it can be for Tourmaline and the industry in the near-term, but looking at it from a medium- and long-term perspective, it can actually be worse for an already weak global economy following the post-pandemic rate hikes, while the fundamentals of oil and gas were already fragile, with production from many countries being at record highs (especially if high prices persist) despite weak demand (which can be even worse due to high prices).

I remain very bullish on Canadian gas, and given the country’s cheap and abundant commodities, weather and potential legislative support, the state and Tourmaline stand to benefit significantly from a potential boom in data center demand. This way, not only would the company not need to search extensively for international premiums, but local commodity prices can help lift these commodities significantly, with AECO prices recently going through a significant slump, which was at 4-decade lows not long ago.

Again, Alberta is already working in this direction, with a dedicated “AI Data Centre Strategy” to potentially attract $100 billion in AI data center infrastructure investments (about a year ago), while the federal government is also reportedly working on something in this direction.

Alongside the country’s “Grand Bargain” MOU regarding the potential pipeline and emission system change, these can help domestic producers reach international markets for better pricing while attracting foreign capital and potentially boosting the local ones as well, which is a step forward for the region.

Especially following Trump’s tariffs and risk for more political pressure, Canada is rightfully looking for new allies, and there’s certainly the possibility of seeing its ties with Asia and Europe deepen over the following years, with a new trade deal with China seen recently, for example.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, the company will always depend on the commodities they produce, which means they will continue to be influenced by politics, wars, weather, etc., with volatility being the only guaranteed thing we have, despite their strong long-term potential.

Meanwhile, potential delays or weaker prices can put pressure on Tourmaline's valuation, as the market will definitely tax them if the major inflection point is delayed even by a bit, as that comes with costs that can affect their balance sheet given their base dividend coverage.

Valuation

For Tourmaline's DCF to Equity, I'll start from their guidance, which I'll reduce quite a bit on purpose to account for a stronger margin of safety, without accounting for net borrowings since I want to highlight their pure potential and they don't need to rely on debt going forward. Note that everything is converted into USD.

That said, let’s assume a FCF of $500 million in 2026, $450 million in 2027, followed by a jump to $1.5 billion in 2028, $1.8 billion in 2029, $1.95 billion in 2030 and $2.2 billion in 2031, followed by a 4% CAGR until year 10 given the country’s and commodity’s long-term potential, followed by a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and a conservative discount rate of 11% to take into account the potential volatility, political pressure and uncertainty this comes with by default, with 388.7 million shares outstanding implied by the per share calculations from the Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $20.37 billion, for an intrinsic value of $52.41 per TRMLF share, or about C$71.13 for TOU:CA, which is above the current $46.18 and C$62.77, respectively, despite using conservative assumptions.

Conclusions

Tourmaline Oil remains a Strong Buy, backed by significant organic potential despite near-term pressure, standing to benefit from their ongoing developments and long-term macro tailwinds.

There are certainly risks, and volatility will definitely persist, but the long-term potential of the company, the commodity and even the country can offset them significantly, potentially playing a very important role in our future.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
